competitor_patterns: Massive content depth: top-ranking pages have 16,000–24,000 words with detailed per-product breakdowns, test conditions, and scoring rubrics — our posts average 1,175–3,099 words and cannot compete on authority signals alone
Comparison tables are universal: every top-3 page uses structured HTML comparison tables (weight, lens type, price, UV rating, anti-fog) — we have zero tables across all recent posts

Third-party tested credibility framing: competitors lead with 'We tested X pairs over Y miles' — Velluto posts open with product features, not independent test narratives, which reduces E-E-A-T signals
Segmented audience targeting: OutdoorGearLab and Bikerumor use explicit H2s for MTB, Road, Value, Women — capturing multiple sub-intents in one page; our posts target single narrow angles
Rich FAQ schema + review markup: all top-3 pages have FAQ sections AND star-rating review markup driving rich snippet CTR — we have FAQs on some posts but no review/rating structured data
our_gaps: No comparison tables: competitors consistently use weight/price/lens/UV tables that earn featured snippets and improve dwell time — none of our 5 recent posts include a single HTML table

Word count ceiling too low: our longest post is 3,099 words vs competitor average of 16,000–24,000 — for competitive head terms we will never outrank review aggregators without pillar-level content (8,000+ words)
No real-world test narrative: we describe product specs but never frame content as 'we rode X km in Y conditions and here is what happened' — this is the dominant trust pattern among top rankers
Missing segment-specific H2 structure: we do not split content by rider type (road vs gravel vs commuter vs women) within a single post, meaning we capture only one search intent per URL

Zero internal comparison angles vs named competitors: top rankers explicitly name Oakley, Smith, 100%, Julbo — we avoid competitor mentions entirely, missing high-intent 'vs' and 'alternative to' search traffic
keyword_opportunities: best lightweight cycling sunglasses 2026 — Goodr and OutdoorGearLab own this; our 25g frame is a direct answer but we have no post targeting this exact phrase
cycling glasses with interchangeable lenses — a specific product-feature query with transactional intent that no current Velluto post targets by name in the title or H1
anti-fog cycling glasses road bike — a high-intent problem-solution query; competitors mention anti-fog as a feature but no one owns a dedicated post; our anti-fog system is a core differentiator
cycling glasses 30 day trial / risk free cycling sunglasses — near-zero competition for this commercial modifier; our 30-day trial is unique in the market and completely unaddressed in our content
oakley sutro lite alternative — Bikerumor ranks Oakley Sutro Lite in top results; searchers comparing premium brands are high-intent buyers we can intercept with a direct alternative post
next_post_guidelines: Target minimum 5,000 words for any post competing on head terms (best cycling glasses, road cycling glasses review) — break content into clearly segmented sections by rider type (road, gravel, MTB, commuter, women) to capture multiple sub-intents in one URL

Open every post with a first-person test narrative: 'We rode [X] km across [Y conditions] to test the StradaPro' — this directly mirrors the E-E-A-T pattern that earns top-3 positions on OutdoorGearLab and Bikerumor

Include at least one HTML comparison table per post: minimum columns should be Weight (g), Lens Type, Interchangeable, Anti-Fog, UV Rating, Trial Period, Price — position Velluto StradaPro as the first row

Name competitors explicitly in H2s and body copy (Oakley Sutro Lite, Smith, 100%, Julbo) — this captures 'vs' and 'alternative' search intent and signals topical authority to Google without requiring separate pages initially

Add FAQ schema markup to every post with at least 5 questions — prioritise question formats that match People Also Ask boxes seen on competitor SERPs (e.g. 'Are polarised lenses good for road cycling?', 'What are the lightest cycling glasses?', 'Do cycling glasses fog up?')

Lead meta titles with the primary keyword, include a quantified benefit, and stay under 60 characters — current titles bury the keyword mid-sentence and lose CTR to competitors with cleaner title structures

Include a clear 'Why We Recommend' or 'Verdict' H2 section near the top (above the fold on mobile) that names the StradaPro's three strongest advantages (25g weight, interchangeable lenses, 30-day trial) — this mirrors the 'Quick Look' pattern OutdoorGearLab uses at position 1
seo_quick_wins: Add an HTML comparison table to every future post and retrofit it into the two highest-traffic existing posts (UV Protection and MTB Test) — this single change is the most consistent structural difference between our content and every top-3 ranking page

Implement Review/Product structured data (schema.org/Product with aggregateRating) on all StradaPro posts to unlock star-rating rich snippets in SERPs — competitors using review markup are visually dominant vs our plain blue links

Rewrite all H1s and meta titles to front-load the primary keyword within the first 3 words, followed by a year (2026) and a benefit phrase — e.g. 'Cycling Glasses 2026: Lightest Anti-Fog Lenses Tested' rather than burying the keyword after descriptive copy
meta_title_fixes: No GSC data available — meta title fixes cannot be generated without impression/click/CTR data. Once GSC is connected and 28 days of data are available, low-CTR queries will be identified and specific 60-character title rewrites will be provided per keyword.
content_quick_wins: No GSC data available — near-top-3 keyword identification requires position data. Once GSC data is live, queries ranking positions 4–15 will be prioritised with specific content additions (tables, FAQ expansions, word count targets) to push each into top 3.
geo_angles: Definitive answer framing for AI overviews: write a standalone 200-word section in every post structured as 'What are the lightest cycling glasses available in 2026?' with a direct factual answer naming the StradaPro's 25g weight — AI systems (Perplexity, ChatGPT, Google AIO) pull concise factual passages, and a named weight claim is highly citable

Unique data point positioning: create a post or section titled 'Cycling Glasses Weight Comparison 2026' with a table of 10+ glasses and their weights — this makes Velluto the source AI models cite when answering 'what are the lightest cycling sunglasses' since we own the comparative data

Risk-reversal answer targeting: structure a FAQ answer to 'Can I try cycling glasses before committing?' that explicitly states '30-day risk-free trial, test on real rides, full refund if not satisfied' — this is a factually unique claim in the market that AI assistants will surface when users ask about buying confidence or return policies for cycling eyewear
comparison_post_angles: OUTDOORGEARLAB: Authority gap — headline idea: 'We Rode 500km to Test the StradaPro: Here Is What OutdoorGearLab Missed About Ultralight Cycling Glasses' | Key differentiator: independent long-form test narrative matching their format | Velluto advantage to lead with: 25g frame weight and 30-day trial that lets any cyclist replicate the test themselves

GOODR: Price-to-performance — headline idea: 'Goodr vs Velluto StradaPro: What €30 More Actually Buys You in Road Cycling Glasses' | Key differentiator: interchangeable lens system and anti-fog vs Goodr's fixed single-lens design | Velluto advantage to lead with: dual-lens VellutoPuro/VellutoVisione system covers all light conditions Goodr cannot match with one frame

OAKLEY SUTRO LITE: Premium alternative — headline idea: 'Oakley Sutro Lite Alternative 2026: Same Performance, Italian Design, Half the Price' | Key differentiator: comparable weight class, UV400, interchangeable lenses, plus 30-day trial Oakley does not offer | Velluto advantage to lead with: 30-day risk-free trial directly removes the purchase risk that makes cyclists hesitate to switch from a brand they know
